facts.text: Sunity Devee was born in 1864 at Sen's House in Coolootola, Calcutta.
Sunity Devee was born during a storm that occurred when she was six days old.
Sunity Devee's father was Keshub Chunder Sen.
Sunity Devee's mother was a member of a strict Hindu family.
Keshub Chunder Sen is considered one of the most remarkable men India has ever produced.
Sunity Devee's mother was gentle, loving, and self-denying.
Keshub Chunder Sen founded the Brahmo religion, also called the Religion of the New Dispensation.
The Brahmo religion is a religion of tolerance and charity.
Keshub Chunder Sen suffered for his religious beliefs.
Sunity Devee's father was earnest and devout from his youth.
Keshub Chunder Sen's conversion meant partial separation from his nearest and dearest.
Sunity Devee's mother was converted to the Brahmo faith by Maharshi D. Tagore.
Sunity Devee's mother gave up caste, rank, money, and jewels to follow her husband.
Sunity Devee's father took her mother to the house of Maharshi Debendra Nath Tagore.
Sunity Devee's family lost caste due to their conversion to the Brahmo faith.
No Hindu servant would work for Sunity Devee's family initially due to their loss of caste.
Sunity Devee's father had a happy nature that enabled him to rise above social discomforts.
Sunity Devee's eldest brother was named Karuna and was named by Maharshi Debendra Nath Tagore.
Sunity Devee's father started a female education movement.
Sunity Devee's father established an Asram where his followers from different classes lived in disregard of caste and class.
Sunity Devee spent many happy days at the Asram near Coolootola.
Sunity Devee had a sister-in-law named Miss Kastogir who was the ideal of her girlhood.
Sunity Devee spent happy days at a Belghuria garden-house lent to her father by a friend.
Sunity Devee was not allowed to pluck fruit or flowers in the Belghuria garden.
Sunity Devee's father was tall and broad-shouldered.
Sunity Devee's father had eyes described as 'homes of silent prayer.'
Sunity Devee's father had wavy hair and wonderfully white even teeth.
Sunity Devee's father always had a smile on his face.
Sunity Devee's father's expression was said to be like that of Buddha, calm and quiet.
Sunity Devee's father's voice was gentle yet clear and could be heard distinctly by large crowds.
Lord Dufferin remarked to Sunity Devee that her father was the most handsome man he had ever seen.
Sunity Devee's husband said her father's foot would be an excellent model for a sculptor.
Sunity Devee's father was indifferent to caste.
Sunity Devee's father disagreed with Maharshi Debendra Nath Tagore over the question of caste.
Sunity Devee's father left Maharshi Tagore due to disagreement over caste and the Maharshi's jealousy.
Sunity Devee's father had powerful influence over his teachings and followers.
Sunity Devee's father lived at one time in Monghyr with his followers.
Sunity Devee's father told stories from the Bible and other sacred books to his children.
Sunity Devee was impressed by her father's story of the Ten Virgins.
Sunity Devee's father told his children a story about four poor Brahmin brothers and a Maharajah.
Sunity Devee was the second of ten children.
Sunity Devee was named after Sunity, the mother of Dhruba.
Sunity Devee attended Bethune College.
Sunity Devee's eldest brother was called 'Dada'.
Sunity Devee went to school and returned home at four o'clock.
Sunity Devee had a second bath after returning from school.
Sunity Devee loved her school.
Sunity Devee was very fond of Miss Hemming, one of her teachers.
Sunity Devee once went to school despite having a high fever and disobeying her mother.
Sunity Devee's household at Coolootola included fifty relations.
Sunity Devee experienced a loss of caste.
Sunity Devee was never afraid of her grandmother.
Sunity Devee's grandmother was very handsome and was the head of the house.
Sunity Devee's grandmother was an excellent cook.
Sunity Devee loved the afternoon hour when she bathed, dressed her hair, and wore muslin saris.
Sunity Devee sometimes conveyed love-letters between temporarily separated married couples.
Sunity Devee made vows to give money to the poor, never tell a lie, feed animals and birds, and give people cool beverages during hot weather.
Sunity Devee's father held a service in the hall every morning.
Sunity Devee's father's motto was 'Faith, Love, and Purity'.
Sunity Devee's father formed a Normal School for girls called the Native Ladies' Normal School.
Sunity Devee's father fought for female education.
Sunity Devee's father established a college in Calcutta named after Queen Victoria.
Sunity Devee's father did not believe in the importance of university degrees for women.
Sunity Devee's father believed it was better for a woman to be a good wife and mother than to have university degrees.
Sunity Devee's father lived at Sadhankanan, a country house not far from Calcutta.
Sunity Devee's mother was small with tiny hands and feet.
Sunity Devee's mother had dark hair and a lovely voice.
Sunity Devee's mother sang hymns and Bengali songs.
Sunity Devee's mother was a woman of strong convictions.
Sunity Devee's mother was a charming story-teller.
Sunity Devee's mother's gentle influence kept the family very much together.
Sunity Devee's mother loved Sadhankanan.
Sunity Devee's mother would never countenance anything her conscience told her was wrong.
Sunity Devee lived at Coolootola in a large house with six courtyards and deep wells.
Sunity Devee lived in the zenana part of the establishment where strict purdah was observed.
Sunity Devee played hide-and-seek in the courtyards and old house.
Sunity Devee admired her pretty cousins who did woolwork slippers for their husbands.
Sunity Devee believed some people married to get unlimited supplies of soap and scent.
Sunity Devee said she would get married to have lovely perfumes and soap.
Sunity Devee played in the front courtyard of her home.
Sunity Devee witnessed open air operas called jatras performed in a field close to the house.
Sunity Devee's father's youngest brother would nail down shutters if he thought jatra acting was improper for ladies to hear.
Sunity Devee prepared lessons in both English and Bengali with her mother.
Sunity Devee prayed with her mother in a small room next to their bedroom.
Sunity Devee was taught mottoes including 'Always speak the truth' and 'Respect and obey your parents'.
Sunity Devee felt she suffered because she disobeyed her mother by going to school with a high fever.
Sunity Devee had vivid memories of the happiness of her childhood.
Sunity Devee found the great house at Coolootola like an enchanted palace.
Sunity Devee was too young to understand what 'conscience' sake' meant.
Sunity Devee felt like an outsider in festivities due to her loss of caste.
Sunity Devee soon threw aside her troubles because everyone at Coolootola was very fond of her.
Sunity Devee used to run about the zenana.
Sunity Devee's grandmother had a warm corner in her heart for her.
Sunity Devee wondered whether she would be like the other ladies when she grew up.
Sunity Devee loved the memory of the afternoon hour on the roof.
Sunity Devee associated perfume and soap with her married cousins.
Sunity Devee felt as if she were living in another world at the little house on the rock.
Sunity Devee's childish heart wondered what love could be that made people careless of life.
Sunity Devee later learned that the agony of fire is nothing compared with the fierce flames of aching remembrance.
Sunity Devee learned that the pang of death is happiness compared with waiting to rejoin a beloved husband.
Sunity Devee does not admire the modern upbringing of children.
Sunity Devee admired the old system's ideas of simplicity and duty.
Sunity Devee found religion never uninteresting and lessons a pleasure.
Sunity Devee traveled by train and in box-like horse carriages.
Sunity Devee traveled from Agra to Jaipur in a box-like horse carriage.
Sunity Devee made a journey to Jubbalpore.
Sunity Devee first realized the devotion of Indian wives to their husbands during a journey to Jubbalpore.
Sunity Devee's mother had a vision of a massacre in an old public building at Etawah.
Sunity Devee's mother could not sleep in the old building at Etawah due to a feeling of horror.
Sunity Devee's mother saw soldiers in red uniforms and Indians struggling together in her vision.
Sunity Devee's mother saw great pools of blood and weeping women and children in her vision.
Sunity Devee's mother thought her vision was only a dream at first.
Sunity Devee's mother saw the vision as vividly with her eyes open as closed.
Sunity Devee's father had not told her mother about the massacre at Etawah lest she be nervous.
Sunity Devee's mother insisted on moving into another house after learning about the massacre.
Sunity Devee's family left Etawah after her mother's experience.
Sunity Devee's family made religious excursions starting from Coolootola.
Sunity Devee and her family always delighted in religious journeys.
Sunity Devee and her family enjoyed 'seeing things' on their journeys.
Kaka Babu, a missionary, had charge of the money and arranged details of their everyday life.
Kaka Babu took care of Sunity Devee's eldest brother and her.
Sunity Devee's father's life was a pilgrimage of extraordinary faith.
Sunity Devee's father trusted in the infinite mercy of God even in the darkest hour.
Sunity Devee's father viewed the failings of others with perfect charity and compassion.
Sunity Devee's father kept the lustre of his private life undimmed to the last.
Sunity Devee's father formed an Asram near Coolootola consisting of two houses joined together.
Sunity Devee's father lived at the Asram with many followers as one big united family.
Sunity Devee's father's followers addressed each other as sisters, aunts, uncles, and brothers.
Sunity Devee's father's way of living as one big united family was unheard of in India at that time.
Sunity Devee's father established the Native Ladies' Normal School.
The Native Ladies' Normal School was at one time the only institution of its kind in India.
Sunity Devee's father was keenly opposed by leading men regarding female education.
Some men who opposed female education were the first to bring their wives out of purdah.
Sunity Devee's father established Victoria College in Calcutta.
Victoria College was named after Queen Victoria.
Victoria College is associated with the name of Keshub Chunder Sen.
Sunity Devee's father taught only things likely to be useful to girls at Victoria College.
Zenana ladies came to lectures at Victoria College.
Miss Pigot was the head of an institution close to where Sunity Devee's family lived.
Miss Pigot's institution trained Christian Indian girls to visit Hindu houses and give lessons.
Miss Pigot took charge of Hindu ladies while their husbands were in England.
Miss Pigot showed the greatest interest in Sunity Devee's family.
Miss Pigot called Sunity Devee's grandmother 'Mother'.
Miss Pigot was still alive at the time of writing.
Sunity Devee was very fond of Miss Pigot.
Miss Pigot was a true friend to Sunity Devee's family.
Sadhankanan was a country house belonging to Sunity Devee's father.
Sadhankanan was not far from Calcutta.
Sadhankanan had a small house but charming grounds with beautiful trees, flowers, and fruit.
Sunity Devee's family lived an open-air life at Sadhankanan among the flowers.
The gardens at Sadhankanan were full of snakes.
A frog jumped onto Sunity Devee's father's knees while being chased by a snake.
The snake stopped and turned away when the frog jumped onto Sunity Devee's father's knees.
Sunity Devee thought her father was more than human after witnessing the snake incident.
Sunity Devee believed the weakest thing would be safe with her father.
No creature ever appealed to Sunity Devee's father's pity in vain.
Sunity Devee's father was a religious man who prayed with his followers.
Sunity Devee's father was compassionate and would never refuse an appeal to his pity from any creature.
Sunity Devee's mother was small with tiny hands and feet and dark hair.
Sunity Devee's mother had a lovely voice.
Sunity Devee's mother was a woman of strong convictions who would not countenance anything her conscience told her was wrong.
Sunity Devee's mother lived for Sunity Devee's father and his beliefs.
Sunity Devee's mother never seemed distressed by her loss of caste.
Sunity Devee's father's faith was expressed in the words: 'You cannot impede my work, for it is God's work.'
Sunity Devee found the greatest consolation in religion during her youth and at the time of writing.
Sunity Devee preferred simple and direct appeal to God as a father and friend over fierce fanaticism or appeals to the senses.
Sunity Devee and her siblings loved the religious services.
Sunity Devee and her siblings were kept quite apart from the world.
Light talk and unkind gossip were unknown to Sunity Devee and her siblings.
Sunity Devee was content with ordinary muslin saris and did not see the beauty of foreign goods.
Sunity Devee's family was very hospitable in the East.
Sunity Devee was always very much attached to her eldest brother Karuna.
Sunity Devee called her eldest brother Karuna 'Dada.'
Sunity Devee and her brother Karuna attempted to make soap together as children.
Sunity Devee's youngest uncle was asked to be a partner in the soap-making scheme.
Coolootola was Sunity Devee's playground.
Sunity Devee believed the rooms at Coolootola had histories.
A staircase at Coolootola was reported to be haunted.
Sunity Devee's father always cooked his own breakfast.
Sunity Devee considered it a great privilege to help her father cook breakfast during holidays.
Sunity Devee experienced a frightening sensation of being followed by cats on the haunted staircase.
Sunity Devee was like a boy and ran and jumped when playing.
Sunity Devee's sister Bino was slim and delicate.
Sunity Devee's sister Bino was sweet and did not scold Sunity Devee when Sunity Devee locked her on the roof.
All of Sunity Devee's brothers and sisters followed their father's teachings throughout their lives.
Sunity Devee's eldest brother was very religious and carried on her father's work.
Sunity Devee's eldest brother's wife copied many of her father's prayers.
Sunity Devee's eldest brother's wife taught in the Victoria College.
Sunity Devee's second brother Nirmal is amiable and easy-going.
Sunity Devee's second brother Nirmal works in the India Office in London.
Sunity Devee's second brother Nirmal works for the welfare of Indian students in London.
Sunity Devee's second brother Nirmal is very popular and always ready to help others.
Sunity Devee's second brother Nirmal is very happy in his home life.
Sunity Devee's second brother Nirmal married a Miss Luddhi.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo was wonderfully gifted.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo was an affectionate friend to her when she was a bride in Calcutta.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo accompanied her to England with her children when her husband could not go.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo was her children's favourite uncle.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo fell ill when a few weeks old and the doctors gave him up.
Sunity Devee's father touched Profullo's face with a rose and Profullo recovered.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo had pet names: Pepery, Peter, Pip, and Peroo.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo married an English girl.
Sunity Devee's third brother Profullo was a devoted follower of her father.
Sunity Devee's husband said her fourth brother Saral inherited her father's great gifts and devoutness.
Sunity Devee's fourth brother Saral said as a small boy he would carry on her father's work.
Sunity Devee's fourth brother Saral is unselfish, kind-hearted, and simple-minded.
Sunity Devee's fourth brother Saral's pet name is Bhopal.
Sunity Devee's youngest brother Subrata's pet name is Bhajan.
Sunity Devee's youngest brother Subrata was her eldest son's best friend.
Sunity Devee's youngest brother Subrata is now a doctor.
Sunity Devee's youngest brother Subrata married a pretty French girl.
Sunity Devee's youngest brother Subrata did well during the War but did not obtain a permanent post.
Sunity Devee's second sister Savitri is quiet and retiring.
Sunity Devee's second sister Savitri's pet name is Bino.
Sunity Devee's second sister Savitri is tall and handsome.
Sunity Devee's second sister Savitri is the best of wives and a very good mother.
Sunity Devee's second sister Savitri married a cousin of Sunity Devee's husband.
Sunity Devee's second sister Savitri has worked hand in hand with Sunity Devee in Cooch Behar.
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu is most unselfish.
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu was engaged to the Maharajah of Mourbhanj when quite a girl.
The Maharajah of Mourbhanj's family came between him and Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu.
The Maharajah of Mourbhanj married a Hindu girl instead of Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu.
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu suffered for several years as an 'old maid.'
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu loved the Maharajah of Mourbhanj throughout and never said an unkind word.
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu said: 'It is my fate, don't blame him.'
Fourteen years passed before Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu found happiness with the Maharajah of Mourbhanj.
The Maharajah of Mourbhanj's first wife died and he came back to ask Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu to marry him.
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu married the Maharajah of Mourbhanj in Calcutta.
The Maharajah of Mourbhanj was accidentally shot at a shooting party.
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu's life was darkened by the Maharajah's death.
Sunity Devee's third sister Sucharu lives for her children and stepchildren.
Sunity Devee's fourth sister Monica is very handsome.
Sunity Devee's fourth sister Monica prayed that luxury might never come into her life.
Sunity Devee's fourth sister Monica is called Moni.
Sunity Devee's fourth sister Monica has the most happy, contented disposition.
No one has ever heard Sunity Devee's fourth sister Monica utter an unkind word.
Sunity Devee's fourth sister Monica's faith in God is wonderful.
Sunity Devee's fourth sister Monica married Professor Sadhu Mahalanobis.
Sunity Devee's youngest sister Sujata has always made sunshine in their midst.
Sunity Devee's youngest sister Sujata is as sweet as a lovely flower.
Sunity Devee's youngest sister Sujata's one idea is to give everyone as much pleasure as she can.
Sunity Devee's youngest sister Sujata was so pretty that the Prince of Wales asked about her.
Sunity Devee's youngest sister Sujata married Mr. Sen, brother of her fourth sister-in-law.
Sunity Devee was not considered a pretty child.
A great-uncle said to Sunity Devee's mother: 'This little girl, Sunity, will be somebody one day, for I see a lotus in her eyes.'
When Sunity Devee was twelve she thought she would make a vow never to marry.
Sunity Devee's ambition was to be clever, to travel a great deal, and to be a sort of nun.
Sunity Devee asked a school friend named Kamari to promise not to marry.
Sunity Devee's school friend Kamari said: 'It is too hard a vow to take.'
Nuns from Loretto Convent visited Sunity Devee's father's school.
A nun from Loretto Convent asked Sunity Devee if she would like to be a nun.
Sunity Devee frequently visited Loretto Convent.
Sunity Devee admired and loved the nuns at Loretto Convent.
Sunity Devee goes to see the Convent Sisters whenever she gets an opportunity.
Sunity Devee believes that Providence gives compensations for sorrows.
Sunity Devee's brothers and sisters have always been most kind and loving to her.
Sunity Devee believed that Western foreigners misunderstood Indian religious symbolism and sacred meanings.
Sunity Devee was proud of India's women and believed they merited India's pride.
Sunity Devee's cousin married a rich young man when she was a little girl.
Sunity Devee's cousin's husband died after a few years, leaving no child.
Sunity Devee's cousin chose to live as a poor woman in her father's house after her husband's death.
Sunity Devee's cousin ate only one meal of vegetables at mid-day.
Sunity Devee's cousin slept under a single blanket during cold months and on a coarse mat in hot weather.
Sunity Devee's cousin prayed for hours daily.
Sunity Devee's cousin was lovely to behold and beloved by everyone.
Sunity Devee's cousin lived her life as a tribute to her husband's memory.
Sunity Devee's family thought more of New Year's Day than Christmas Day, probably because that was her father's custom.
Sunity Devee's father opened the Sanctuary at Lily Cottage and preached his last sermon on New Year's Day.
Sunity Devee's family regarded New Year's Day as more important after losing her father.
Sunity Devee's husband was the only Maharajah who never had nautch girls or actresses at his Court.
Sunity Devee's husband had four sons.
Sunity Devee had three sons in Indian costumes and one son named Rajey in Royal Yeomanry uniform.
Sunity Devee's happy home life continued undisturbed until she was thirteen.
Sunity Devee's father's name is associated with the Civil Marriage Act.
Sunity Devee's father exerted efforts that led the Government to pass the Civil Marriage Act fixing the marriageable age at eighteen for men and fourteen for girls.
Sunity Devee was married to Nripendra Narayan Bhup Bahadur, Maharajah of Cooch Behar.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar had been a ward of the Government since his infancy.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar was carefully educated to be a model ruler.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar was descended from one of the oldest ruling families in the country.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar was removed to the Wards' institution at Benares when he was five years old.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar was removed from Benares to Patna when he was eleven.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar became a student at Government College in Patna.
Mr. St. John Kneller became the Maharajah's tutor and guardian in 1872.
The Maharajah remained in Patna for five years.
The Maharajah attended the Durbar at Delhi in 1877 when the Queen was proclaimed Empress of India.
The Maharajah was presented with the Kaisar-i-Hind medal by Lord Lytton.
The Maharajah was saluted with thirteen guns for the first time at the Delhi Durbar.
The Maharajah had become a clever young man and a keen sportsman.
The Maharajah was fond of his native soil and people.
Sir Richard Temple was the Lieutenant-Governor of Bengal.
The Rajah's mother and grandmother expressed anxiety about the Rajah visiting England.
The Palace ladies feared that after seeing Europe, the Rajah would not care for Cooch Behar or his relatives.
The Rajah's mother and grandmother maintained that only as a married man could the Maharajah go away from India with security.
The Government hoped the Maharajah would take only one wife.
A party from Cooch Behar arrived in Calcutta in search of a bride.
Mr. Chuckerbutty sought advice from Prosonna Babu, one of Sunity's father's missionaries.
Jadab Babu spoke of Sunity as a potential bride.
Mr. Chuckerbutty initially doubted that the Minister's daughter would become Maharani.
Sunity's father was very surprised when the marriage was first suggested.
Sunity's father never gave thought to worldly or family affairs.
Sunity's father refused the marriage offer initially.
The Government and State representatives continued urging Sunity's father to consent to the marriage.
Sunity's father repeatedly refused the marriage proposal.
Sunity's father said in a letter that Sunity was neither very pretty nor highly educated.
Sunity's father eventually became persuaded that the marriage was a Divine command.
Sunity's second sister Bino remarked that their father and mother were talking about marriage.
Sunity initially thought the discussion was about a young missionary's marriage.
Sunity fell ill with fever the day before the officials arrived from Cooch Behar.
Sunity's father told her that officials were coming to see her, not the school.
Sunity's father told her she might marry a handsome young Maharajah.
Marriage was an undiscussed subject for Sunity before this revelation.
Sunity felt very shy when told about the marriage prospect.
Sunity's mother gave her lovely jewels and a mauve and gold sari for the meeting.
Sunity was given a strong dose of quinine before meeting Mr. Dalton.
Sunity played a simple piece of music on the piano for Mr. Dalton.
Mr. Dalton wrote that he thought Sunity a very charming young lady and suitable bride for the Maharajah.
On January 27, 1878, Mr. Dalton wrote that the Lieutenant-Governor decided the Rajah should go to England in March.
Mr. Dalton wrote that the Rajah should be married before going to England.
The Rajah initially expressed distaste for being married.
The Rajah was averse to being worried about marriage and wished to remain single until of age to live with his wife.
The Rajah came to see that an educated bride was not to be procured elsewhere.
The Rajah became eager for the alliance with Sunity.
The Rajah's mother's consent to the marriage was secured with great difficulty.
The marriage was to be a solemn betrothal rather than a marriage in the ordinary sense.
The Rajah was to proceed to Europe immediately after the ceremony.
The Rani's condition was that someone other than Sunity's father should give away the bride.
The objection to Sunity's father giving away the bride was based on the fact that he had been to England.
The marriage was set for March 6.
Sunity had not yet completed her fourteenth year at the time of the marriage.
The Dewan sent a telegram saying the Deputy Commissioner could not wait too long for assurances of consent.
The Maharajah wrote to Sunity's father expressing his belief that no man should take more than one wife.
The Maharajah identified himself as a Theist who believed in one true God.
Sunity's sister told her the Maharajah was very handsome and very clever.
When Sunity first met the Maharajah, she was extremely nervous.
The Maharajah brought a present for Sunity in his carriage.
The Maharajah offered the present as a token of his love.
From the moment the Maharajah offered the present, Sunity and her future husband loved each other.
Sunity and the Maharajah met several times but always in the presence of others.
Sunity knew the Maharajah loved her despite limited private interaction.
There were constant worries concerning religious rites during marriage preparations.
The State people would not hear of a Brahmo alliance.
Sunity declared she would not change her religion.
Sunity said she loved the Maharajah and would marry no one else.
Priests from Cooch Behar came to Sunity's house and promised the marriage would be arranged as her father wished.
Sunity's father came to have an affection for the Maharajah.
Sunity's whole family fell captive to the Maharajah's charm and kindness.
The Maharajah clearly liked Sunity's family and had deep respect and admiration for her father.
The ceremony of 'Jurini' took place when Sunity's marriage was settled.
The Maharajah sent saris to all of Sunity's relations.
The Maharajah distributed more than a hundred plates of sweetmeats to the household.
Hostile criticism gathered regarding Sunity's father's motives in allowing the marriage before the stipulated age.
Sunity's marriage preliminaries were a stormy time in her life.
People questioned Sunity's father's motives without full faith in him and his doctrines.
The Government was determined on the marriage.
Sunity's father could have published the correspondence but chose not to.
Sunity's father remained undismayed by attacks made upon him.
Sunity's father said he became a Brahmo by Divine call and gave consent to the marriage by the same Divine command.
Sunity's father said he obeys God, not man.
The Dewan, Calica Das Dutt, was the Prime Minister of Cooch Behar.
The Dewan was not in favour of the marriage because he thought he had been ignored.
The Dewan was an influential follower of Sunity's father.
The marriage correspondence went through Jadab Babu, who was only a junior officer.
Mr. Dalton sought to avoid unnecessary display of Brahmoism at the wedding.
The Rajah's relatives at Cooch Behar were still wedded to old superstitions.
The Dewan opposed the marriage because he thought he had been ignored.
The Dewan was an influential follower of Sunity Devee's father.
Jadab Babu was a junior officer who handled correspondence relating to the marriage.
Mr. Dalton wrote to Jadab Babu about arrangements for the wedding.
Mr. Dalton wanted to avoid unnecessary display of Brahmoism at the wedding.
The Rajah was making a great concession to enlightened ideas by marrying a Brahmo girl.
Mr. Dalton disapproved of the idea of bridesmaids at the wedding.
Babu Keshub Chunder Sen was a prominent Brahmo leader.
The wedding party left Calcutta on 25th February, 1878.

Sunity Devee's wedding party consisted of her father, mother, grandmother, father's sister, his younger brother, his special followers, two ladies who were wives of missionaries, some relations, and a girl friend.
Sunity Devee was not permitted to see the Maharajah until her wedding day.
Sunity Devee received State officials seated on a carpet and accepted coins from them according to tradition.
The Dewan and Raj pandits discussed the wedding ceremony with Sunity Devee's father the day before the wedding.
Sunity Devee's father refused to consent to parts of the ceremony according to old Hindu rites.
There was a dispute about whether the marriage should take place before or after the Maharajah's departure for England.
Sunity Devee wore a pale blue sari with raised gold flowers and a bright red satin veil created by a French dressmaker.
The Maharajah wore clothing that was one mass of gold from head to foot.
The wedding ceremony was performed by Rev. Gour Govind Roy, a missionary of the Brahmo Church.
The Maharajah's Hindu priests were present at the wedding ceremony.
The Maharajah threatened to ride away from Cooch Behar for good if he could not marry Sunity Devee.
The Maharajah said he would marry no one if he could not marry Sunity Devee.
Mr. Dalton brought a written agreement to Sunity Devee's father confirming the wedding would take place according to Brahmo rites.
The Lieutenant-Governor telegraphed that the marriage be performed according to the rites as settled in Calcutta.
The wedding ceremony took place at 3 a.m.
The wedding was celebrated in an enormous tent.
The Maharajah followed close behind Sunity Devee as she was carried on a piece of wood between lines of soldiers.
The palace ladies threatened and scolded Sunity Devee after the wedding.
The palace ladies insisted that Sunity Devee must become a Hindu.
The Maharajah had gone to Darjeeling with Mr. Dalton before departing for England.
The Maharajah arranged to join Sunity Devee's train and travel part of the way back to Calcutta.
Most of Sunity Devee's father's followers raised objections to her marriage.
Some of Sunity Devee's father's followers threatened to kill him.
Followers of Sunity Devee's father left the Church one after another.
Dissident followers built a church known as Sadharan-Somaj.
A member of the Sadharan-Somaj published a book called 'The History of the Brahmo-Somaj.'
Sunity Devee believes the book published by the Sadharan-Somaj member contains untruths.
Sunity Devee learned from babyhood that truth conquers untruth.
Many of Sunity Devee's father's followers insisted that her marriage had been a Hindu ceremony.
Sunity Devee's marriage was not an idolatrous ceremony.
Sunity Devee became Maharani of Cooch Behar.
Maharshi Debendra Nath Tagore and other Brahmos opposed the Brahmo Act that Sunity Devee's father wished the Government to pass.
The Tagores called themselves Brahmos but wanted their marriage ceremony to be known as Hindu marriage (non-idolatrous).
The Brahmo Act was not passed due to opposition from other Brahmos.
Sunity Devee's father was a Brahmo leader.
Sunity Devee's marriage to the Maharajah of Cooch Behar was arranged by the British Government.
Sunity Devee's father believed the marriage should be conducted under Brahmo rites rather than Hindu rites.
Sunity Devee's father opposed other Brahmos like Maharshi Debendra Nath Tagore who wanted their marriages recognized as Hindu ceremonies.
Sunity Devee's marriage was recognized by the Government and the State as a Hindu marriage.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar was a Brahmo but ruled a Hindu Raj.
Sunity Devee's father believed he acted as a public man guided by conscience and divine duty in accepting the marriage proposal.
Sunity Devee's father received pressure from the British Government to make concessions regarding the marriage ceremony.
Sunity Devee lived at Lily Cottage in Calcutta with her family.
Sunity Devee attended a prayer-room at Lily Cottage every morning before breakfast.
Sunity Devee's father picked flowers from the garden each morning for the prayer-room.
Sunity Devee's mother arranged flowers in the sanctuary at Lily Cottage.
Sunity Devee had a governess named Miss S⸺ who was a society woman but ineffective as a teacher.
Sunity Devee wanted to learn history and languages but her governess only emphasized German.
Sunity Devee's governess had a strong Scottish accent and a trying temper that frightened and repelled her.
Mrs. White taught Sunity Devee painting.
Sunity Devee's sister-in-law taught her Bengali, which became the only language she knew well.
Sunity Devee corresponded frequently with her husband while he was in England.
The Maharajah returned to India in February 1879.
The Maharajah joined Presidency College in Calcutta in 1879 and lived on Theatre Road.
Sunity Devee was to remain at Lily Cottage until she was sixteen and the Maharajah was eighteen.
The Maharajah was fond of Sunity Devee's mother and enjoyed stealing her keys as a playful prank.
The Maharajah respected missionaries and treated Sunity Devee's siblings as his own.
The Maharajah was full of praise for England and was a loyal British subject.
The Maharajah was Western in his ideas but entirely Indian at heart.
Sunity Devee and the Maharajah attended church every Sunday morning.
Sunity Devee and the Maharajah were married in the Church of the New Dispensation when she was sixteen and he was eighteen.
The Maharajah gave Sunity Devee a turquoise and diamond ring as a wedding gift.
Sunity Devee and the Maharajah spent their honeymoon at Burdwan.
Sunity Devee was treated as a strict purdah lady at Burdwan, with the railway station covered to prevent outsiders from seeing her.
The palace at Burdwan overlooks a lake.
The Maharajah of Burdwan owned a zoological garden near the honeymoon apartments.
The Dowager Maharani of Burdwan gave Sunity Devee sovereigns as a welcome gift instead of a customary present.
The Dowager Maharani of Burdwan worshipped her late husband's slippers.
Sunity Devee was initially not allowed to meet Indian gentlemen, including her husband's cousins.
The Maharajah objected to Sunity Devee meeting Indian men who did not bring out their own wives.
The Maharajah was only two years older than Sunity Devee.
The Maharajah was a very strict husband.
Sunity Devee always respected her husband.
Sunity Devee liked the Raj-Kumari, the daughter of the late Maharajah of Burdwan.
Sunity Devee found all the palace ladies at Burdwan to be good-looking.
Sunity Devee's honeymoon was very happy.
At first Sunity Devee was not allowed to meet Indian gentlemen, not even her husband's cousins.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar objected to Sunity Devee meeting men who did not bring out their own wives.
Sunity Devee always respected the Maharajah as if he had been years older.
Sunity Devee considered the Maharajah her hero and ideal husband.
The Maharajah was very particular about Sunity Devee's dress.
The Maharajah disapproved of Sunity Devee wearing pearls next to her skin.
The Maharajah did not like loud laughter or loud talking.
Sunity Devee was not allowed to ride, dance, or play tennis.
The Maharajah allowed his daughters to be 'outdoor' girls because he did not know what sort of men they might marry.
Woodlands was bought and beautifully furnished as a place near Calcutta for Sunity Devee and the Maharajah.
Sunity Devee was delighted with her little sitting-room at Woodlands, which was decorated in pale pink and blue.
Although the Maharajah liked English food and lived like an Englishman, Sunity Devee was faithful to Indian cookery.
Sunity Devee gave two enjoyable dances at Woodlands and was quite a successful hostess.
The Maharajah took great pride in Sunity Devee.
The Maharajah would tell Sunity Devee to never forget whose daughter she was.
Sunity Devee's father's influence was entirely responsible for any good she may have done in her life.
In the summer of 1881 Sunity Devee was expecting to become a mother.
For many generations no heir had been born to the ruler of Cooch Behar's chief wife.
The succession to the throne of Cooch Behar had always been through the son of a wife of lower rank.
While staying in Mussoorie, a fortune-teller told Sunity Devee she would have a son who would rule the country.
Sunity Devee's father had a premonition about the expected baby.
Sunity Devee's father said 'A Sebak (a devotee) is coming from God' while at prayers a few weeks before the child arrived.
Sunity Devee's first son was born on the 11th April, 1882 at Woodlands.
Sunity Devee suffered greatly during the birth of her first son.
For a couple of hours after the birth began, Sunity Devee's relations did not know if she would survive.
Sunity Devee's first son possessed the 'tika' (a prominence on the forehead), which is said to be bestowed only on very powerful rulers.
Sunity Devee's father was alone in his room deep in prayer when told that a prince was born to Cooch Behar.
Money and sweets were lavishly distributed to celebrate the birth of Sunity Devee's first son.
Sunity Devee's husband came into her room and sat by her bedside after the birth of their son.
Sunity Devee's husband whispered 'Darling wife, we have a little boy' when he came to see her after the birth.
Sunity Devee and her husband were very happy after the birth of their son.
Sunity Devee's father named the child Raj Rajendra (King of kings), but he was always called Rajey.
Rajey's birth was celebrated with great public rejoicings both at Calcutta and Cooch Behar.
On the seventeenth day after Rajey's birth, Sunity Devee gave an evening party where presents were bestowed on the baby.
Woodlands was illuminated on the day of Rajey's naming celebration.
Rajey was a perfectly behaved baby who hardly ever cried.
Rajey was so fair that he was nick-named 'the English baby.'
The Maharajah allowed Sunity Devee to nurse Rajey herself, a privilege not often permitted to Royal mothers.
Everyone adored Rajey.
Father Lafont, a Jesuit Father in Calcutta, always alluded to Rajey as 'my boy.'
When Sunity Devee arrived at Cooch Behar for Rajey's naming ceremony, public feeling had completely changed in her favor.
As the mother of a Prince, Sunity Devee became popular both in palace and State.
Milk had to be well guarded for the heir, and sentries stood all round the cow when it was milked.
Some of Sunity Devee's happiest days were spent at Simla.
Rajey often went to see Sunity Devee's father, who had a house at Simla.
Sunity Devee's youngest brother was only six months older than Rajey.
Rajey's rank was never put forward, as it was the Maharajah's wish that his son should be brought up as simply as possible.
On the 31st October, 1883, the Maharajah completed his 21st year.
The Maharajah was installed as reigning ruler on the 8th of November 1883 by Sir Rivers Thomson, the Lieutenant-Governor.
Rajey and his father looked splendid together at the installation ceremony.
Sunity Devee's father had been ailing for a few months before his death.
Sunity Devee's father was much reduced by December, 1883.
Sunity Devee's childhood home had been an abode of 'sweetness and light.'
On the 1st of January, 1884, Sunity Devee's father expressed a wish to consecrate the new sanctuary in the grounds of Lily Cottage.
Sunity Devee's father was carried down to the sanctuary despite being exhausted and very ill.
Sunity Devee's father offered his last and most impressive prayers at the sanctuary on the 1st of January, 1884.
Sunity Devee's father breathed his last on the 18th January, just before 10 a.m.
The house, compound, and roads at Lily Cottage were full of people when Sunity Devee's father died.
In the first bitterness of her grief, Sunity Devee felt that she could never know happiness again.
Sunity Devee's father's missionaries and followers were like sheep without a shepherd after his death.
Sunity Devee's mother gave up everything in the way of comfort after her husband's death.
Sunity Devee's mother wore coarse saris and discarded her soft bed after her husband's death.
Sunity Devee's mother slept on a hard, wooden bedstead after her husband's death.
Only the cheapest things were to be seen in Sunity Devee's mother's room after her husband's death.
The Dowager Lady Dufferin was a kind friend to Sunity Devee's mother.
Sunity Devee's father's ashes rest in a mausoleum built in front of the new sanctuary.
Sunity Devee's mother's ashes are near her husband's in the mausoleum.
The ashes of Sunity Devee's brother, his wife and their baby boy are in the mausoleum.
The ashes of Sunity Devee's third brother, Profulla, are in the mausoleum.
After losing her father, Sunity Devee heard wonderful singing in the hush of the hour before twilight.
A tiny plant grew between the crevices in the ground over Sunity Devee's father's grave.
The plant that grew over Sunity Devee's father's grave became a flowering tree with rich clusters of bloom.
The flowering tree blossomed season after season until it was cut down by a mistake of the masons when Sunity Devee's mother's gravestone was being placed.
Eight days after losing her father, Sunity Devee held a little daughter in her arms.
Sunity Devee wondered whether her newborn daughter had met her father's noble spirit on its upward flight.
Sunity Devee's daughter's birth brought some consolation to the house of mourning.
Sunity Devee named her daughter Sukriti (Good deeds), but they always called her Girlie.
Girlie is fascinating rather than lovely.
When Rajey was four years old, the Maharajah's people were most anxious for him to marry again.
The Maharajah's people said if anything happened to Rajey and the Maharajah also died, the throne would have to go to another branch of the family.
The old Maharani and the late Calica Das Duth planned that it was most necessary for the Maharajah to marry again.
The Maharajah never mentioned the matter of remarrying to Sunity Devee.
The Maharajah told the doctor that he would always be over-anxious about Rajey's health until another son was born.
Sunity Devee's second son Jitendra, whom they called Jit, was born on the 20th December, 1886.
Sunity Devee was greatly delighted to find a fine church of the New Dispensation at Cooch Behar.
There was a girls' school at Cooch Behar named after Sunity Devee.
There was much room for improvement in Cooch Behar, although Government had well prepared the way.
The Maharajah was to be a ruler in the highest sense of the word.
Sunity Devee was to win the hearts of the people of Cooch Behar as a woman, a wife, and a mother.
The more Sunity Devee saw of Cooch Behar the more she liked it.
According to old tales, the god Siva chose Cooch Behar as his earthly home on account of its luxuriant loveliness.
Many legends have gathered round Cooch Behar, mostly of the time when it formed part of the kingdom of Kamrupa.
Sunity Devee has often thought how uncharitable the general public are about the failings of those in high places.
Sunity Devee believes it is unfair to judge hastily without knowing the divine object of each life.
Sunity Devee believes people who rashly judge often do grave injustice to those who have been called to a high station.
When Sunity Devee first came to Cooch Behar, they lived in the old palace, which was like a town.
Hundreds of ladies occupied the various houses of the old palace at Cooch Behar.
Sunity Devee's husband's ancient race has been brave soldiers, generous to friend and foe, and passionate lovers.
Sunity Devee believes the public is often unjust in judging rulers and princes without knowing them well.
Sunity Devee believes God has chosen some men to be rulers and others to be subjects.
Sunity Devee lived in the old palace at Cooch Behar when she first arrived, which housed hundreds of ladies.
Sunity Devee now lives in a new palace at Cooch Behar designed by a Western architect in an eclectic style.
The new palace at Cooch Behar has a Durbar Hall, swimming bath, covered racket and tennis courts, and gardens.
Sunity Devee can see the Himalayas from the palace on clear winter days.
Sunity Devee has never seen as many fireflies together as in Cooch Behar.
Sunity Devee had English nurses for all her children except Rajey.
Mrs. Eldridge, an English nurse, expected to find Sunity Devee to be stout, dark, and uneducated.
Sunity Devee's daily routine included making sketches of flowers brought by her children.
Sunity Devee wore a silk sari after her bath, which she considered sacred.
Sunity Devee arranged the prayer-room with roses, jessamine, and bright-hued flowers against white marble.
Sunity Devee prepared fruits and sweets for the household.
Sunity Devee cut vegetables for the household, preparing enough for fifty people.
The household generally had eighteen curries for each meal.
Sunity Devee sliced betel-nut and filled jars with it.
Sunity Devee made sweets because her husband and children were fond of them.
Sunity Devee learned to prepare pickles from her mother.
Sunity Devee made betel leaves into packets filled with spices and pinned with cloves.
Sunity Devee got up tableaux and plays to amuse her relations-in-law and officers' wives.
Sunity Devee was very fond of painting and spent hours ornamenting mantelpieces and glass panes.
Sunity Devee and her family made a miniature garden that the children loved.
Sunity Devee's second son, the present Maharajah, once wanted to bathe in the miniature garden's tank.
The Maharajah devoted himself to plans for the comfort, well-being, and education of his subjects after his accession.
The Maharajah made new roads and developed the State's resources.
The Maharajah erected hospitals, schools, and public buildings.
The Masonic Lodge in Cooch Behar is one of the largest in Bengal.
The Maharajah founded a college of which he was very proud.
Hindu princes are allowed to marry as many wives as they wish.
Maharanis are part of the State and have a different position than other palace ladies.
Maharanis must be young girls of good family.
Sunity Devee has altered her position regarding going out to other houses.
Sunity Devee's mother-in-law was a well-built, short, strong woman who was bright and full of fun.
Sunity Devee's mother-in-law died of cholera at Woodlands.
Rajey, Sunity Devee's cousin, helped nurse her mother-in-law through her last illness.
The Maharajah had a step-brother and step-sister who were both very handsome.
There is a palace custom that no one shall have a meal before the Maharajah.
Sunity Devee kept the custom of not eating before the Maharajah strictly for a few years.
The Maharajah told his mother that the Maharani should have her meals before him if he was delayed.
The Maharajah's mother did not like the change to the meal custom but had to obey.
Until she left India, Sunity Devee had never tasted meat.
Sunity Devee loves Cooch Behar for its abundance of birds and flowers.
The Cooch Behar winters are like those of the South of France.
Sunity Devee endeavored to gain the confidence and affection of her husband's subjects.
Sunity Devee never knowingly ran counter to the prejudices of her husband's subjects.
In Darjeeling and Calcutta, Sunity Devee is considered a Maharani with advanced Western ideas.
In Cooch Behar, Sunity Devee was and is the zenana lady who enters into the lives of the people.
Rajey had large sad eyes, lovely curling hair, and grew into a straight-limbed slender boy.
In January 1884, Rajey sickened with typhoid fever in Simla and became seriously ill.
Doctors declared Rajey's typhoid case to be hopeless.
The Maharajah said he and Sunity Devee would give their lives for Rajey if God would spare him.
After six weeks, Rajey recovered from typhoid fever.
Rajey's nature was sweetly unselfish even as a little boy.
Rajey cried at the sad parts of stories.
Rajey asked how rulers get their money and learned they tax people.
Rajey said he would never ask for taxes until everybody was well off and able to pay.
Rajey once collected his boots and shoes to give to a servant's children.
Rajey was loyal to a degree and believed once a friend, always a friend.
Rajey never spoke unkindly even when deceived or belittled by those he trusted.
Rajey was hit by his bearer, which made everyone indignant.
The Maharajah never permitted anyone to touch the children.
Rajey refused to tell on his bearer despite being questioned by his father.
The Maharajah loved Rajey more because of his loyal affection for his servant.
Rajey cried out against unfairness when the English secretary's younger boy was losing a fight.
Rajey had a strong sense of justice.
Rajey's father was his ideal and he believed no one was as wonderful or good.
Rajey told the doctor that nobody in the world could be taller than his father.
Rajey was impressed with a sense of the responsibilities of those destined to govern.
Rajey seemed to realize the hollowness of earthly state.
Rajey never tired of listening to Indian legends with moral lessons.
The year 1887 was expected to be memorable for India as Queen-Empress would celebrate her Jubilee.
Many Indian princes decided to render homage to the Queen-Empress in person in 1887.
The Maharajah made plans for 1887 long beforehand.
The Maharajah kept his intention for Sunity Devee to accompany him to England secret from everyone.
In 1887, conditions of life among Indian ladies were very different from later times.
The Maharani of Baroda had once gone to Switzerland.
For the wife of a ruler to visit England with her husband caused quite a sensation in 1887.
Sunity Devee believes she was the first Maharani to visit England with her husband.
Sunity Devee dreaded the experience of visiting England.
Sunity Devee knew absolutely nothing about the journey to England.
Sunity Devee was sensitive about being a stranger in a strange land.
Sunity Devee admired Prince William and the way foreign Royalties showed their respect for ladies.
Sunity Devee's husband chose a diamond pendant with an uncut ruby to give to Queen Victoria.
Sunity Devee was nervous about presenting gifts to Her Majesty.
Sunity Devee and her husband traveled to Windsor Castle by special train to present gifts to Queen Victoria.
Sunity Devee presented a gift to Queen Victoria with a few words and made a deep curtsy.
People present said the Maharani of Cooch Behar's words were clear and her curtsy was graceful.
Sunity Devee enjoyed a dinner with Mr. and Miss Kinnaird where the table was decorated with scarlet roses from Paris.
The roses at the dinner reminded Sunity Devee of the old Belghuria garden.
Sunity Devee and her husband were given incorrect dress code information for the Guildhall event.
The Maharajah felt uncomfortable being the only man in evening dress at the Guildhall.
The Prince of Wales noticed the Maharajah's discomfort and sent for him.
The Prince of Wales jokingly told Sunity Devee she was a careless wife for not dressing her husband properly.
The Prince of Wales's kindly tact put the Maharajah at ease.
Empress Frederick asked Sunity Devee about Indian customs and how she addresses her husband.
Sunity Devee addresses her husband in the third person, using the term 'Ogo' meaning 'Please, dear.'
Empress Frederick and others began calling each other 'Ogo' after learning the term.
Empress Frederick complimented Sunity Devee's hair.
Sunity Devee paid her utmost devotion to Queen Alexandra, then Princess of Wales.
Sunity Devee found that punctuality was not considered a virtue in London.
Sunity Devee fainted with the arrival of soup at a dinner due to tiredness.
Sunity Devee saw Letty Lind dance exquisitely at an entertainment.
The Prince of Wales came and sat in Sunity Devee's box at the theatre.
The Prince of Wales accepted an invitation to supper with Sunity Devee and her husband.
The Prince of Wales remarked that the Maharajah of Cooch Behar was a straightforward man.
The Maharajah was straightforward and scorned subterfuges.
The Maharajah was outspoken and never ashamed of his friends regardless of their position.
The Maharajah was proud but simple in nature.
The Maharajah believed his joie de vivre would pass unnoticed.
Queen Victoria wore the pendant that Sunity Devee and her husband had presented to her.
Sunity Devee felt touched seeing Queen Victoria wear their gift.
Queen Victoria had strawberries and fruit prepared for Sunity Devee's children.
Sunity Devee's son Rajey carried away two enormous bunches of grapes from Queen Victoria's table.
Queen Victoria was delighted by Rajey's actions with the grapes.
Emperor Frederick admired Sunity Devee's son Jit and spoke kind words to him.
Sunity Devee saw the Empress of Russia, then Princess Alex of Hesse, as a young girl of sixteen playing with children and dogs.
Sunity Devee thought the Empress of Russia had a lovely face and sad eyes.
The Duchess of Connaught invited Sunity Devee to lunch at Windsor Castle.
Mrs. Bignell accompanied Sunity Devee to lunch with the Duchess of Connaught.
The Duchess of Connaught did not invite anyone but Sunity Devee.
Sunity Devee was touched to find the Duchess of Connaught living like an ordinary mother in her own home.
Sunity Devee attended a grand garden-party at Buckingham Palace with thousands of people.
Queen Victoria walked straight up to Sunity Devee and kissed her at the garden-party.
Sunity Devee believed she was the only woman the Queen kissed at the garden-party.
Newspapers made sarcastic remarks about the Indian Princess receiving more attention than others.
Sunity Devee was commanded to dine with Queen Victoria at Windsor Castle and spend the night there.
A red carpet was laid on Windsor station platform for Sunity Devee's arrival.
The crowd cheered Sunity Devee and her husband at Windsor station.
Sunity Devee found the suite at Windsor Castle splendid yet comfortable.
The sitting-room at Windsor Castle faced the park.
The bedroom at Windsor Castle was gilt from ceiling to floor.
Sunity Devee was puzzled by the lack of an obvious bathroom in the lavishly furnished suite.
The Maharajah found a hidden bathroom by pressing a button-like knob.
Sunity Devee screamed with joy upon discovering the hidden bathroom.
The face towels at Windsor Castle were of the finest linen.
The Maharajah told Sunity Devee she must thank Her Majesty nicely if she received a present.
Sunity Devee was nervous about meeting Queen Victoria in the ante-room.
The Maharajah helped Sunity Devee carry her jewels, bracelets, fan, and gloves.
Sunity Devee and her husband were the first to arrive in the ante-room.
Queen Victoria gave Sunity Devee the decoration C.I., 'Crown of India.'
Princess Beatrice pinned the Crown of India decoration on Sunity Devee.
Queen Victoria's words with the gift meant more to Sunity Devee than the present itself.
Prince Henry of Battenberg was a dark, handsome man.
The dining-room at Windsor Castle was Queen Victoria's own private room.
The dining-room contained portraits of the Queen's three daughters-in-law.
The portrait of the Princess of Wales as a bride was exquisite.
Queen Victoria sat in the middle at dinner, with the Maharajah on her right and Princess Beatrice on her left.
Sunity Devee refused champagne at dinner.
Queen Victoria noticed Sunity Devee's refusal of champagne and asked the Maharajah the reason.
The Maharajah told Queen Victoria that Sunity Devee never took champagne or wine.
Queen Victoria was pleased rather than annoyed by Sunity Devee's abstinence.
Queen Victoria and Princess Beatrice asked after Sunity Devee's mother and grandmother.
Queen Victoria remembered Sunity Devee's father and had a photograph of him.
An Indian Maharajah arrived at dinner and would not have English food.
Princess Beatrice asked why more Indian ladies did not come to England.
Sunity Devee slept little in the gold bed at Windsor Castle.
The suite at Windsor Castle belonged to the Princess of Wales.
There was no marked ceremony at Royal parties and everything was delightfully informal.
The Princess of Wales gave Sunity Devee a lovely Russian cypher brooch at Marlborough House.
Sunity Devee had a tête-à-tête tea with the Princess of Wales and brought her babies.
Sunity Devee's son Jit was attracted to the Princess of Wales.
The Princess of Wales said Jit was a pretty little boy and remembered when he was ill.
Queen Alexandra's kindness to Sunity Devee has never varied since early days.
Sunity Devee and Queen Alexandra have each lost their idolised first-born.
Sunity Devee and Queen Alexandra have each lost the best of husbands.
Sunity Devee believes a subtle sympathy draws her and Queen Alexandra together.
Sunity Devee was struck by Queen Alexandra's resigned expression in recent visits.
Sunity Devee never went into Bond Street shops except her dressmaker and Hamley's toy shop.
Sunity Devee felt like buying the whole of Hamley's toy shop.
Sunity Devee enjoyed the theatres.
Sunity Devee remembered Shakespeare's 'Winter's Tale' with Mary Anderson.
Mary Anderson's lovely face and good acting impressed Sunity Devee.
Sunity Devee attended an opera that reminded her of the Indian love story 'Nal and Damayanti.'
Queen Victoria lent her box to Sunity Devee for an opera.
Madame Albani sang at the opera Sunity Devee attended.
Sunity Devee was much interested in the British Museum.
Sunity Devee could have spent days looking at things in the British Museum.
Sunity Devee attended the Naval Review and saw boats decorated with lights.
Sunity Devee visited the Royal stable arranged by Lord Elphinstone.
Sunity Devee's son Rajey was put in the Royal coach at the Royal stable.
Sunity Devee visited Madame Tussaud's and was delighted with the figures.
Sunity Devee did not have the courage to go into the Chamber of Horrors.
Sunity Devee was impressed by a large picture of the late Prince Imperial with Zulus attacking him.
Sunity Devee visited the Tower of London and was much interested.
Sunity Devee visited Edinburgh and found it a lovely town.
Sunity Devee's eldest brother had told her the story of Edinburgh castle in childhood.
Sunity Devee visited Holyrood Palace.
The guide at Holyrood Palace showed Sunity Devee every room and corner, relating stories that took hours.
The Maharajah grew tired during the Holyrood Palace tour.
The guide brought bits of old paper from Queen Mary's time at Holyrood Palace.
The Maharajah became impatient when shown the old paper.
It rained and looked dark and dismal during Sunity Devee's visit to Holyrood Palace.
Sunity Devee brought away a sad picture of Queen Mary being beheaded.
Sunity Devee is happy that her country has never had a Queen as cruel as to murder her first cousin.
The Duke of Manchester sat next to Sunity Devee at dinner in London.
Sunity Devee felt sore that her husband had not been given a Jubilee decoration.
Sunity Devee argued with the Duke of Manchester about her husband deserving recognition.
Sunity Devee compared her husband's situation to a boy at school not receiving promotion.
The Maharajah had done more than any other Ruler to improve his State.
The Duke of Manchester suggested Sunity Devee talk to the Prince of Wales.
The Maharajah cabled that Queen Victoria graciously conferred on him the G.C.I.E.
Sunity Devee was sorry the decoration was not the G.C.S.I.
Sunity Devee believed Queen Victoria meant to give the G.C.S.I.
Sunity Devee and her husband went to Brighton for the Goodwood Races.
Sunity Devee and her husband went to the races every day.
Sunity Devee enjoyed the drives up the hills at Brighton.
Cooch Behar is a very small State.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar did more than any other Ruler to improve the condition of his State.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar was conferred the G.C.I.E. by Her Majesty.
Sunity Devee wished the Maharajah had been given the G.C.S.I. instead of the G.C.I.E.
Sunity Devee and the Maharajah went to Brighton for a few days for the Goodwood Races.
They attended the races every day and enjoyed the drives up the hills.
The Maharajah was absent for the first two days to pay a promised visit to an old friend.
The Prince of Wales expressed a wish to be introduced to a cousin of the Maharajah's.
The Prince of Wales liked Indian dishes.
The Prince of Wales acquired his taste for Indian dishes from a memorable visit to India.
King Edward had a wonderful personality.
King Edward remembered things well and spoke kindly of India.
Sunity Devee was asked to many dances in London.
Sunity Devee enjoyed the dances in London.
The dances were full of pretty faces and dresses.
Sunity Devee saw Lady Randolph Churchill wearing a maroon-coloured velvet dress.
Prince George of Wales pointed out Lady Randolph Churchill to Sunity Devee.
At the Duchess of Leeds' ball, Sunity Devee was engaged to dance with the Earl of Durham.
The Earl of Durham had a twin brother.
Sunity Devee mistook the Earl of Durham's twin brother for the Earl.
Lord Abergavenny had twin daughters named Lady Violet and Lady Rose Neville.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar mistook one of Lord Abergavenny's twin daughters for the other.
Sunity Devee attended a violin concert.
Sunity Devee found the violin concert memorable.
Dozens of pretty girls dressed in white played melodies at the violin concert.
Sunity Devee attended a grand dance at Lady Revelstoke's.
Sunity Devee enjoyed the dance at Lady Revelstoke's.
Lady Ripon and her brother were very tall and remarkably good-looking.
Lady Abergavenny gave a dance.
Lord and Lady Headfort were very kind to the Maharajah and Sunity Devee.
Lord and Lady Headfort were the parents of the present Lady Suffield.
Sunity Devee and the Maharajah attended an afternoon party at Hatfield House.
Hundreds of guests travelled to Hatfield House by a special train.
The Maharajah of Cutch asked Sunity Devee and the Maharajah of Cooch Behar to get into his carriage.
The Maharajah of Cutch is of higher rank than the Maharajah of Cooch Behar.
The Maharajah of Cutch asked Sunity Devee to sit beside him.
Sunity Devee wanted to sit next to her husband.
Newspapers described Sunity Devee as the Maharani of Cutch.
Sunity Devee was annoyed by the newspaper description.
The Maharajah of Cooch Behar regarded the newspaper mistake as a joke.
The Princess of Wales suggested that Sunity Devee see the Maze at Hatfield House.
Sunity Devee had never ventured inside a maze before.
Sunity Devee followed Princess Victoria and Princess Hélène of Orleans into the maze.
Sunity Devee, Princess Victoria, and Princess Hélène of Orleans became lost in the maze.
They ran screaming deeper into the maze.
They broke through the hedges to find an exit.
Their dresses were torn to ribbons from breaking through the hedges.
Sunity Devee and the Maharajah attended King Edward's Coronation at Hatfield.
Lord Salisbury was the host at Hatfield during the Coronation visit.
Lord Salisbury remembered the Maharani of Cooch Behar who lost herself in the maze in 1887.
Lord Salisbury complimented Sunity Devee on appearing much younger than expected.
A woman at the garden-party remarked that Sunity Devee was pretty.
Another woman said Sunity Devee had not only a pretty face but also a good face.
Sunity Devee considered the compliment about her good face to be the most appreciated compliment paid to her.
Lady Salisbury was always very kind to Sunity Devee.
Sunity Devee dined at Lady Salisbury's before an India Office party.
Lady Salisbury took Sunity Devee to the India Office party in her cee-spring carriage.
Sunity Devee forgot what kind of carriage a cee-spring carriage was and felt shy.
Lady Salisbury's kindness helped Sunity Devee enjoy the India Office party enormously.
Sunity Devee attended an evening party at the Duchess of Teck's.
The Duke of Mecklenburg-Strelitz was blind.
The Duke of Mecklenburg-Strelitz was the husband of Her late Majesty's first cousin.
